coredata.dc:title: Signal-on photoluminescent detection of dopamine with carbon dots-MnO2 nanosheets platform based on inner filter effect
coredata.dc:creator: Tan, Xin
Zhang, Pu
Ye, Cuiying
Min, Yuanhong
Li, Qianwen
Wang, Yi
coredata.dc:description: Development of new methods for dopamine (DA) assay has attracted continuous attention because it serves a vital role in clinical treatment of neurological diseases such as depression, schizophrenia, and Parkinson's disease. Herein, we design a carbon dots (CDs)-MnO2 nanosheets platform for rapid and sensitive detection of DA in pharmaceutical products. The photoluminescence of CDs can be quenched by MnO2 nanosheets via inner filter effect (IFE) because the absorption of MnO2 nanosheets fully covered the excitation and emission bands of CDs. However, DA can transform MnO2 nanosheets into Mn2+ ions, resulting in the elimination of the IFE and recovery of the photoluminescent signal. This sensing platform shows a selective and sensitive response to DA in the range of 1–30 μM, with a detection limit of 100 nM. The presented IFE-based strategy gives a new insight on development of label-free, selective and sensitive optical methods for drug assay.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence
Manganese dioxide
Quantum dots
Optical sensor
Pharmaceutical analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of MnO2 NSs and CDs
Synthesis and characterization of CDs and MnO2 NSs
Sensing strategy for DA and mechanism studies
Analytical performance of the IFE-based platform for DA detection
Detection of DA in pharmaceutical products

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21775014 and 81972020 ) and Open Foundation Project of Engineering Research Center for Biotechnology of Active Substances , Ministry of Education (No. AS201905 ). Y.W. and P.Z. were sponsored by the Chongqing High-level Personnel of Special Support Program and Chongqing Talent Program , respectively. X.T. and Q.L. were also supported by the National Undergraduate Training Program for Innovation and Entrepreneurship of China (No. 201910637005 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterizations of the fluorophore and absorber of the IFE sensing platform: (A) TEM image of the CDs; (B) TEM image of the MnO2 NSs; (C) photograph of a typical fluorescent pattern obtained by printing the CDs on a PET membrane and irradiating under a 365-nm UV lamp; (D) Fluorescence spectra of CDs (line a, excitation; line b, emission) and UV–vis absorption spectrum of MnO2 NSs (line c). Inset of (D) is the corresponding solution of the CDs under UV (left) and visible (right) light.

Fluorescence emission spectra of (a) CDs, (b) the mix of CDs, MnO2 NSs and DA, and (c) CDs-MnO2 NSs mixture. The inset shows a photograph of the corresponding solution under 365-nm UV lamp. Conditions: MnO 2 NSs, 20 μg/mL; DA, 25 μM; pH, 6.6; reaction time, 3 min.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs, MnO 2 NSs, DA, CDs-MnO 2 NSs mixture, MnO 2 NSs-DA mixture, and CDs-MnO 2 NSs-DA mixture. (B) Dynamic light scattering results of CDs, MnO2 NSs, CDs-MnO2 NSs mixture, and CDs-MnO2 NSs-DA mixture. Conditions: MnO 2 NSs, 20 μg/mL; DA, 25 μM; pH, 6.6; reaction time, 3 min.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of CDs in the presence of different amount of MnO2 NSs (from 0 to 35 μg/mL) at pH 6.6. Inset shows the plot of fluorescent intensity against the concentration of MnO2 NSs. (B) Effect of MnO2 NSs concentrations on the fluorescence quenching and restoration of the system for DA detection (DA, 30 μM; pH, 6.6; reaction time, 3 min). (C) Effect of pH on the fluorescence quenching and restoration of the system for DA detection: (a) CDs, (b) CDs-MnO2 NSs mixture, and (c) CDs-MnO2 NSs with DA (MnO 2 NSs, 20 μg/mL; DA, 25 μM; reaction time, 3 min). (D) Reaction kinetics of the platform for DA detection: (a) CDs, (b) CDs-MnO2 NSs with DA, and (c) CDs-MnO2 NSs mixture (MnO 2 NSs, 20 μg/mL; DA, 25 μM; pH, 6.6; time record, 10 min).

(A) Fluorescence spectra of CDs-MnO2 NSs platform in the presence of different concentrations of DA (from bottom to top: 0, 1.0, 5.0, 10.0, 15.0, 20.0, 25.0, and 30.0 μM). (B) Linear dependence of the fluorescence restoration efficiency (F/F 0) on the concentration of DA. The error bars were obtained from three repetitive measurements.

The effects of potentially interfering substances on the fluorescent detection of DA. The concentration of DA standard solution in each sample was 30 μM, and 1.5 mM of other potentially interfering substances were added together with DA (50 mg/mL for proteins and polysaccharides). The error bars were obtained from three repetitive measurements. Substances 1–26 were CH3COONa, Na2PO4, Na2C2O4, Na2S2O3, KCl, trisodium citrate, EDTA, ZnSO4, MnSO4, Mg(NO3)2, citric acid, mercaptosuccinic acid, glucose, saccharose, starch, α-cyclodextrin, β-cyclodextrin, dextrin, lactose, carboxymethylcellulose, vancomycin, bovine serum albumin, glutathione, tiopronin, uric acid, and mannitol, respectively.
Schematic illustration of the CDs-MnO2 NSs fluorescent platform for DA assay based on inner filter effect (IFE).
Results for the assay and recoveries of DA in injections.

head.ce:title: Signal-on photoluminescent detection of dopamine with carbon dots-MnO2 nanosheets platform based on inner filter effect

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Engineering Research Center for Biotechnology of Active Substances, Ministry of Education, Chongqing Key Laboratory of Green Synthesis and Applications, College of Chemistry, Chongqing Normal University, Chongqing, 401331, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Engineering Research Center for Biotechnology of Active Substances
Chongqing Key Laboratory of Green Synthesis and Applications

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Engineering Research Center for Biotechnology of Active Substances, Ministry of Education, Chongqing Key Laboratory of Green Synthesis and Applications, College of Chemistry, Chongqing Normal University, Chongqing, 401331, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chongqing Key Research Laboratory for Drug Metabolism, College of Pharmacy, Chongqing Medical University, Chongqing, 400016, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chongqing Key Research Laboratory for Drug Metabolism

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chongqing Key Research Laboratory for Drug Metabolism, College of Pharmacy, Chongqing Medical University, Chongqing, 400016, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Development of new methods for dopamine (DA) assay has attracted continuous attention because it serves a vital role in clinical treatment of neurological diseases such as depression, schizophrenia, and Parkinson's disease. Herein, we design a carbon dots (CDs)-MnO2 nanosheets platform for rapid and sensitive detection of DA in pharmaceutical products. The photoluminescence of CDs can be quenched by MnO2 nanosheets via inner filter effect (IFE) because the absorption of MnO2 nanosheets fully covered the excitation and emission bands of CDs. However, DA can transform MnO2 nanosheets into Mn2+ ions, resulting in the elimination of the IFE and recovery of the photoluminescent signal. This sensing platform shows a selective and sensitive response to DA in the range of 1–30 μM, with a detection limit of 100 nM. The presented IFE-based strategy gives a new insight on development of label-free, selective and sensitive optical methods for drug assay.

ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine (DA) is one of the crucial neurotransmitters which belongs to the catecholamine and phenethylamine families. It plays important roles as a chemical messenger in central nervous system, cardiovascular, hormonal and renal systems in human bodies [1–3]. Trace level change in concentration of DA is associated with various disorders such as Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease, Huntington's disease, schizophrenia and epilepsy [4–6]. As a clinical drug, DA injection (Intropin) can be used to treat certain conditions that occur when people are in shock, which may be caused by heart attack, trauma, surgery, heart failure, kidney failure, and other serious medical conditions. Different dosages of DA injection are used in clinical treatment of the diseases caused by different reasons [7,8]. The difference is also seen between usual adult dose and pediatric dose. Therefore, rapid quantification of DA is vital for diagnosing the related diseases as well as quality control of DA in pharmaceutical products for clinical treatment.

To this end, a great number of different analytical methods including high-performance liquid chromatography [9,10], capillary electrophoresis [11], colorimetry [12–14], fluorometry [15–18], chemiluminescence [19,20] and electrochemical methods [21–23] have been developed for the detection of DA. Nevertheless, the intrinsic defects of different methods usually limit their applications. For example, the chromatography and capillary electrophoresis usually require expensive equipment and/or complicated operations as well as time-consuming. The major limitation of electrochemical methods is the potential interferences from the molecules such as glucose, uric acid and ascorbic acid with high concentration in biological samples [24]. With the distinct advantages of high sensitivity, selectivity, easy operation, and diversity of the available probes, the fluorescence-based approaches are a good alternative for the detection of DA. Fluorescent sensors can be developed through different mechanisms including photoinduced electron transfer, electronic energy transfer, metal−ligand charge transfer, intramolecular charge transfer, twisted intramolecular charge transfer, and fluorescence resonance energy transfer [25]. Generally, intermolecular interaction between the fluorescent probe and target molecule are essential to achieve the detection since the fluorescence can only change when they keep close enough or have a complementary geometry [26]. For this purpose, complicated modification of the fluorescent probes usually needs to carry out before their usage in detection, which is time-consuming and cost-increasing.

Inner filter effect (IFE), which results from the absorption of the excitation and/or emission light of a fluorophore by a specific absorber in a system, is an alternative strategy to design fluorescent sensing platform [27–30]. Modification of the probes is needless because the distance between the fluorophore and absorber is not strictly required. Moreover, high sensitivity can be achieved with this strategy since the changes in absorbance of the sensors transform exponentially into the changes of fluorescent intensity. A high efficiency of the IFE needs a good spectral overlap between the absorption band of the absorber and the excitation and/or emission band of the fluorophore [31,32]. Thus, it is important to choose a suitable pair of fluorophore and absorber to establish the IFE-based fluorescent sensors. In this work, carbon dots (CDs) with strong blue fluorescence and two-dimensional MnO2 nanosheets (MnO 2 NSs) with large molar extinction coefficient are employed as the fluorophore and absorber of IFE, respectively. The absorption of MnO2 NSs fully covers the excitation and emission bands of CDs, resulting in the fluorescence quenching of the CDs. However, with the addition of DA, the IFE can be inhibited and thus the fluorescence of CDs is recovered due to the decomposition of MnO2 NSs by DA. Based on this mechanism, a signal-on fluorescent sensing method is developed for DA, which has been successfully applied in the quality control of DA in pharmaceutical products.

Polyethylenimine (PEI, MW = 70000, 50%), manganese chloride tetrahydrate (MnCl2·4H2O, 99.99%), and tetramethylammonium hydroxide (TMA·OH) were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Dopamine and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30%) were obtained from Shanghai Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) and Kelong Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Chengdu, China), respectively. Citric acid and methanol were purchased from Chuandong Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Chongqing, China). All chemicals were analytical-reagent grade and used without further purification. Phosphate buffer solution (pH 6.2) was used in DA detection. Ultrapure water with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ cm was used throughout the experiments.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded by a Tecnai G2 F20 transmission electron microscopy (FEI, USA). Fluorescent spectra were measured through an LS-55 fluorescence spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA). UV–vis absorption spectra were measured using an UV-2550 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) data were obtained by a Nano Brook omni (Brookhaven, USA). A portable WFH-204B UV lamp (Yuhua Instrument, China) with 365-nm UV light was used to excite the sample to get fluorescent photographs. Fluorescent patterns were printed using a modified MP288 printer (Canon, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of MnO2 NSs and CDs
ce:section.ce:para: MnO2 NSs were prepared following the protocol in our previous work [33]. Briefly, 10 mL of MnCl2·4H2O (0.3 M) was rapidly added into an aqueous solution (20 mL) containing 0.6 M TMA·OH and 3 wt% H2O2. The mixture was stirred vigorously overnight at room temperature, and the precipitate was collected through centrifugation, washing and drying. Then, 10 mg of the sample was dispersed in 100 mL water and ultrasonicated for 3 h to obtain MnO2 NSs.

For the synthesis of fluorescent CDs, 0.5 g PEI and 1.0 g citric acid were fully mixed in 10 mL water, and then transferred to a 25-mL Teflon-lined autoclave for reaction at 200 °C. After 4 h, the product was cooled to room temperature and centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min. Supernatant with light yellow color was collected and stored at 4 °C for further use.

The as-prepared CDs were diluted for 10000 times with water before use in DA detection. First, 1.36 mL of phosphate buffer (pH 6.6), 0.4 mL of MnO2 NSs (0.1 mg/mL), and 0.04 mL of DA standard solution with different concentrations were mixed at room temperature. Then, 0.2 mL of diluted CDs were added, and fluorescent spectra were recorded after 3 min. For DA assay in injections, the procedure was similar with the standard procedure, except for the replace of DA standard solution by DA injection. In addition, recovery experiments were also carried out to verify the reliability of this method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CDs and MnO2 NSs
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescent CDs were synthesized through a one-step hydrothermal route at 200 °C, employing citric acid and PEI as carbon and nitrogen sources, respectively. The MnO2 NSs were prepared through the oxidation of MnCl2 by H2O2 in the presence of TMA·OH, where a dark brown solution was obtained. The morphology and structure of the CDs and MnO2 NSs were characterized by TEM imaging. As shown in Fig. 1 A, the CDs are in quasi-spherical shape, monodisperse, and uniform in size. Their average diameter is distributed in the narrow range of 3.0 ± 0.4 nm, which is statistically calculated from more than 100 particles in the TEM images. These results demonstrated that CDs with good water dispersibility were successfully prepared. Fig. 1B demonstrates that ultrathin lamellar MnO2 NSs with occasional folds and wrinkles have been synthesized, which are a typical two-dimensional layer structure.

Optical properties of the CDs and MnO2 NSs were further studied. Fluorescent quantum yield (QY) of the CDs with 58.5% was obtained using quinoline sulfate (QY = 54% in 0.1 M H2SO4) as a reference dye, which was calculated according to the following equation [34]:
φ =
φ '
×
A '
I '
×
I A
×
n 2
n
' 2
where
φ
is the QY,
I
is the integrated emission intensity,
n
is the refractive index and
A
is the optical density of the CDs. The prime symbol (′) refers to the quinine sulfate. It was also observed that the CDs solution displayed light yellow color under visible light, while emitted bright blue fluorescence when they were irradiated by a 365-nm UV lamp (inset of Fig. 1D). In order to further prove the highly luminescence of CDs on solid interfaces, we employed a modified ink-jet printer to make fluorescent patterns through printing the CDs on a transparent polyethylene terephthalate (PET) membrane which nearly has no background fluorescence. Ethylene glycol (15%, v/v) was added into the CDs ink to obtain high-quality and quick-dry patterns. As shown in Fig. 1C, a bright and clear blue fluorescent pattern was observed under UV light, and its fluorescent intensity kept the same after six months in an indoor environment. This result indicates that the as-prepared CDs are promising materials for applications such as photoelectric devices and anti-counterfeiting. Photoluminescent spectra of the CDs show their maximal excitation and emission peaks at 356 and 445 nm, respectively (Fig. 1D). Exactly, the MnO 2 NSs displays a broad absorption in the range of 200−600 nm with a characteristic absorption centered at ~380 nm in UV–vis spectrum. It means that the absorption of MnO2 NSs can not only shield the excitation light for CDs but also absorb the emission light from the CDs. Therefore, the good spectral overlap ensures the occurrence of IFE in a highly efficient way.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing strategy for DA and mechanism studies
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1 illustrates the IFE-based strategy of CDs−MnO2 NSs platform for DA detection, which includes the fluorescent quenching of CDs by MnO2 NSs and the transformation of the MnO2 NSs into Mn2+ in the presence of DA. CDs are employed as the fluorescent signal reporter beyond other photoluminescent materials owing to their advantages of good photostability, low cost, and environmentally friendly nature [35]. As an absorber of IFE, MnO 2 NSs have strong and broad absorption spectral range that overlap with both the fluorescent excitation and emission of the CDs. Moreover, a rather large molar extinction coefficient of 9.6 × 103 L mol−1 cm−1 makes MnO2 NSs efficient absorber for fluorescence quenching in IFE [36]. As shown in Fig. 2 , the CDs solution exhibits a maximum fluorescent emission at 445 nm (curve a). The fluorescent intensity of CDs can be effectively quenched by MnO2 nanosheets (curve c). This result demonstrates the feasibility of IFE between the fluorescent CDs and MnO2 NSs. However, it was found that the fluorescent intensity of CDs was remarkably recovered after adding an appropriate concentration (micromolar level) of DA into the system (curve b). The photograph also shows the enhanced luminescence of the solution in the presence of DA (inset of Fig. 2). These results suggested that MnO 2 NSs were effectively decomposed by DA, and thus the IFE between CDs and MnO 2 NSs was inhibited. Thus, the proposed nanoplatform can be used for DA detection based on the inhibition of IFE and fluorescent recovery of CDs.

It is well known that DA has strong reducing capacity attributed to the two phenolic hydroxyl groups. Therefore, the MnO2 NSs can be reduced to Mn2+ through the redox reaction between MnO2 and DA. Meanwhile, the phenolic hydroxyl groups in DA are transformed into ketones [37]. In order to clarify such a mechanism, UV–vis spectra and DLS under different conditions were measured. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the CDs show a weak absorption peak at 356 nm, which is in accordance with their maximal excitation wavelength. Both MnO2 NSs and CDs-MnO2 NSs mixture have the same broad absorption band centered at 380 nm, suggesting no reaction occurs between CDs and MnO2 NSs. DA has a characteristic peak at 280 nm, which is originated from π–π* transitions as well as p–π conjugate of the electrons from –OH and benzene. After reacting with MnO2 NSs, the absorption band at 280 nm disappears, while two new bands at 300 nm and 470 nm are observed. These features in UV–vis spectra confirmed the oxidation of dopamine by MnO2 NSs indeed occurred in the present system [37,38]. Moreover, the average hydrodynamic sizes of the samples were measured by DLS. Fig. 3B shows that the CDs in solution are 5 nm in size, which is relatively larger that the result obtained from TEM imaging (3.0 ± 0.4 nm). The two-dimensional MnO2 NSs and CDs-MnO2 NSs mixture display their sizes of 125 nm and 134 nm, respectively. However, the size dramatically decreases to 48 nm after the addition of DA into the system. Although the hydrodynamic sizes from DLS cannot give the actual size of the anisotropic 2-D nanosheets, the result clearly reveals the distinct size distributions in the absence and presence of DA [39]. Again, it confirms that DA can reduce MnO2 NSs to Mn2+ ions and thus inhibit the IFE between MnO2 NSs and CDs.

In order to achieve good performance of the IFE-based sensing platform for DA, further studies under different conditions were carried out. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the fluorescent intensity of CDs is gradually decreased along with increasing the concentration of MnO2 NSs. The quenching efficiency can reach ~95% when 35 μg/mL of MnO2 NSs are introduced. Nevertheless, it does not mean the more MnO2 NSs the better performance of the sensor. On the one hand, when a relatively high concentration of MnO2 NSs was introduced, very weak fluorescence restoration was observed in the presence of a fixed amount of DA. On the other hand, less amount of MnO2 NSs were not able to thoroughly quench the fluorescence of CDs, which could cause poor sensitivity. This phenomenon is probably because DA preferentially react with the dissociative MnO2 NSs in solution rather than the combination of MnO2 NSs with CDs. Only an appropriate amount of MnO2 NSs can balance the quenching and restoration of the fluorescent signal. As shown in Fig. 4B, the efficiencies of both fluorescent quenching and restoration exceeded 70% when 20 μg/mL of MnO2 NSs were introduced, which was chosen as the best amount of absorber in the IFE. Fig. 4C shows the fluorescent signal in quenching and restoration processes is relatively stable in the pH range of 4.5–6.5, and pH 5.4 was chosen as the optimal pH for DA detection. In addition, the reaction kinetics under different conditions were also investigated. As shown in Fig. 4D, the CDs display excellent photostability where their fluorescent intensity remains unchanged under sustained irradiation (curve a). After adding only MnO2 NSs (curve c) or the mixture of MnO2 NSs and DA (curve b), either the fluorescence quenching or restoration can reach stable state within 3 min, suggesting that rapid detection of DA can be achieved based on this sensing platform.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the IFE-based platform for DA detection
ce:section.ce:para:
Under optimal conditions, the response of the IFE-based platform to DA was investigated. Specifically, a fixed volume of CDs was mixed with 20 μg/mL of MnO2 NSs and different concentrations of DA (0, 1.0, 5.0, 10.0, 15.0, 20.0, 25.0 and 30.0 μM) for 3 min at room temperature. It was found that the fluorescent intensity was gradually restored along with increasing the concentration of DA (Fig. 5 A). A linear plot of F/F 0 against the concentration of DA over the range of 1–30 μM was obtained (detection limit: 100 nM), where F 0 and F corresponds to the fluorescent intensity before and after the addition of DA into the detection system, respectively (Fig. 5B). The linear regression equation was F
/
F
0 = 0.09610
c + 1.007 (
R
2 = 0.9965), where c is the concentration of DA.

Selectivity is a significant character to study the performance of the IFE-based sensing platform, which is essential for a new method before its applications in quality control of DA-based formulations. Therefore, the influence of potentially interfering substances and excipients in pharmaceutical products on DA detection was studied, including inorganic cations (Na + , K + , Mg 2+ , Zn 2+ and Mn 2+ ), anions (Cl − , PO 4 3− , SO 4 2− , S 2 O 3 2− , NO 3 − , C 2 O 4 2− , CH 3 COO − and citrate), saccharides and their derivates (glucose, lactose, saccharose, dextrin, α-cyclodextrin, β-cyclodextrin, starch, mannitol and carboxymethylcellulose), peptides/proteins (glutathione and bovine serum albumin), other drugs (vancomycin, tiopronin and mercaptosuccinic acid), citric acid, uric acid, and EDTA. As shown in Fig. 6 , after adding the above substances (50.0 mg/mL for protein and polysaccharide, and 1.5 mM for others) to DA standard solution (30.0 μM), the fluctuation of fluorescent intensity of the CDs−MnO2 NSs system stays less than 10%. In addition, the detection process can be completed within 3 min by this strategy, which will save us a lot of time when a mass of samples needs to assay. Since the IFE-based platform is dependent on the redox reaction between DA and MnO 2 NSs, strong antioxidants normally existed in blood or urine (e.g., ascorbic acid) will also have response and thus interfere the DA detection. Hence, this method can be used for quality control of DA in pharmaceutical products while is not suitable to detect DA in biological samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of DA in pharmaceutical products
ce:section.ce:para: To examine the practical application of the proposed IFE-based method, determination of DA in injections was further explored. As the results summarized in Table 1 , the detected concentrations of DA in injections were very close to the labelled amount of the medicine. Under the optimal detection conditions, the recovery experiments were also carried out to evaluate the accuracy and reliability of this method. Three different concentrations of DA standard solution (2.0, 5.0 and 10.0 μM) were added to the injections, and recoveries in the range of 102.0–110.1% were obtained. These results indicated that the IFE platform based on CDs-MnO2 NSs system was reliable and feasible for quality control of DA in pharmaceutical products.

In summary, we have successfully established a new IFE-based fluorescent strategy for DA sensing, in which CDs as an IFE fluorophore and MnO2 NSs as an IFE absorber. The detection was achieved through the fluorescence “switch-on” in the presence of DA, resulting from the redox reaction between DA and MnO2 NSs and thus the decomposition of the IFE absorber. The proposed method was proved to be simple, time-saving and economical, which provides a potential platform for quality control of DA in clinical drugs. Since complex modification of the fluorophore or covalent binding between a fluorophore and a quencher is needless to ensure their intermolecular interactions, the IFE-based strategy in this study would open a new avenue for the development of facile and rapid analytical methods and extend to other systems for sensing.

Xin Tan: Investigation, Visualization, Writing - original draft. Pu Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Cuiying Ye: Investigation, Visualization. Yuanhong Min: Visualization. Qianwen Li: Validation. Yi Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Writing - original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21775014 and 81972020) and Open Foundation Project of Engineering Research Center for Biotechnology of Active Substances, Ministry of Education (No. AS201905). Y.W. and P.Z. were sponsored by the Chongqing High-level Personnel of Special Support Program and Chongqing Talent Program, respectively. X.T. and Q.L. were also supported by the National Undergraduate Training Program for Innovation and Entrepreneurship of China (No. 201910637005).
